metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step (e.g., a `scripts/verify_mcp_publisher_pin.py` or a dedicated AC) that downloads the exact pinned URL, computes its SHA-256, and asserts it matches the value embedded in `release.yml` — so the correctness of the pinned hash (not just the verification mechanism) is validated in-session before the change lands.
evidence: Plan AC7: 'Verified by `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py`, which corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits non-zero' — this proves the mechanism, not the correctness of the embedded hash value
Plan scope: 'hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time with a rationale comment' — no step verifies the computed-and-embedded value is self-consistent with the pinned URL
ABSENCE: No AC or step describes downloading the pinned URL and comparing its digest against the value stored in release.yml (a `curl <pinned-url> | sha256sum` cross-check)
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that the SHA-256 value embedded in release.yml for the mcp-publisher archive is actually correct for the pinned download URL. `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` tests only that the sha256sum verification *mechanism* exits non-zero when a byte is corrupted (using a test-generated archive), not that the workflow-embedded hash matches the real binary at the pinned URL. An implementer who embeds a wrong SHA-256 (e.g., copied from the wrong arch, typoed, or stale) will see all tests pass — the defect surfaces only at release runtime when the real download occurs.
findings.location: AC7 / `tests/unit/test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` description / Scope bullet for `mcp_verify`+`mcp_registry` split
scenarios: Implementer pins version v1.2.3 but copies SHA-256 from the v1.2.2 tarball — all static YAML tests and mechanism tests pass; actual release workflow fails at sha256sum -c
Implementer pins the darwin/amd64 hash instead of linux/amd64 — same silent pass in tests, runtime failure only
Upstream renames the asset after the plan is written — embedded URL 404s; static tests still pass
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Running `python scripts/verify_mcp_publisher_pin.py` (or equivalent `make verify-mcp-pin` target) downloads the exact pinned URL, computes its SHA-256, and asserts byte-for-byte equality with the hash stored in `release.yml`; this script is run once by the implementer as part of the PR and its output is attached to the Gerrit change or the release ticket.' This is a one-time in-session command the implementer runs to confirm the embedded hash is self-consistent with the pinned URL — it bridges the gap between mechanism testing and hash-value correctness without requiring a full release run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the installed version of `zizmor` has a check that specifically flags absent `persist-credentials: false` on checkout steps (consult zizmor's changelog/docs); if it does not, add `persist-credentials: false` as a `test_release_workflow_pins.py` assertion (check list item alongside the existing rebar-specific checks) so the invariant has a confirmed enforcement path.
evidence: Plan states: 'zizmor enforces full-SHA pinning of uses:, persist-credentials: false, and over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC) on release.yml'
AC: 'Every release checkout sets persist-credentials: false — enforced by zizmor (via make lint), which flags credential persistence'
zizmor's published check catalogue does not prominently list a persist-credentials check; this is a load-bearing unverified capability assertion
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `zizmor` detects absent `persist-credentials: false` on checkout steps, but this is an unverified capability claim. zizmor's documented checks (unpinned-uses, ref-confusion, excessive-permissions, dangerous-triggers, etc.) do not prominently include a `persist-credentials` linter. If zizmor lacks this check, the enforcement guarantee stated in AC line 2 ('enforced by zizmor (via make lint), which flags credential persistence') silently fails — no lint failure would occur if `persist-credentials: false` were removed.
findings.location: AC line 2 / 'What' section: 'zizmor enforces ... persist-credentials: false'
scenarios: A future PR removes persist-credentials: false; zizmor does not flag it; the AC is silently unmet despite make lint passing
The plan's layered enforcement model collapses to only the test_release_workflow_pins.py assertion (if zizmor lacks the check), which the plan currently does NOT list as a fallback for this invariant
findings.suggested_fix: Add `persist-credentials: false` to the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` rebar-specific assertions as a belt-and-suspenders fallback regardless of zizmor's capability, since the cost is negligible and the plan already parses `release.yml` YAML in that test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalizing the plan, verify that the pinned `zizmor` version detects absent `persist-credentials: false` on `actions/checkout` steps (e.g. check `zizmor --help` / its audit-rule catalog / GitHub issue tracker) and document the exact audit category or flag required; if zizmor does not cover this, add a `test_release_workflow_pins.py` assertion as the enforcement fallback.
evidence: AC2: 'Every release checkout sets persist-credentials: false — enforced by zizmor (via make lint), which flags credential persistence.'
Plan Scope: 'zizmor enforces the generic checks: full-SHA pinning of every uses:, persist-credentials: false, and over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC).'
zizmor is not installed in the repo environment (UNRESOLVED from resolve_symbol); its exact rule catalog for persist-credentials detection cannot be confirmed from plan text or repo files.
Evidence class: Unverified — could not probe zizmor --help or rule documentation.
findings.finding: The plan designates `zizmor` as the sole enforcement mechanism for AC2 ('persist-credentials: false on every release checkout — enforced by zizmor (via make lint), which flags credential persistence'), but `zizmor`'s ability to detect absent `persist-credentials: false` on `actions/checkout` steps is an unverified specific capability. zizmor primarily audits unpinned `uses:` references and dangerous permission scopes; whether it emits a finding for missing `persist-credentials: false` depends on its rule catalog and may require a specific audit category flag or may not be implemented. If this detection is absent, AC2 has no enforcement mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Scope — 'zizmor enforces … persist-credentials: false'; What section — 'zizmor enforces full-SHA pinning of uses:, persist-credentials: false, and over-broad/dangerous permissions'
scenarios: zizmor is pinned and installed but its rule set does not include a `persist-credentials: false` check — AC2 silently passes even if a checkout lacks the flag.
zizmor detects this only with a non-default audit profile or flag not named in the plan — CI runs with the default invocation and misses the check.
findings.suggested_fix: Run `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` against the current file (which has no `persist-credentials: false`) and confirm it emits a finding for that missing field. If it does not, add a `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check asserting `persist-credentials: false` is present on each checkout step, and remove the 'enforced by zizmor' claim from AC2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pinned install path for `pip-tools` (e.g. `pipx install pip-tools==<version>` in `docs/releasing.md` "Pinning" section, mirroring the `git-cliff` pattern) or add it to `pyproject.toml [dev]` with an exact pin, so the lock-regeneration command is reproducible.
evidence: Plan: 'generated and maintained with an established tool — pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools) (or uv pip compile --generate-hashes) — checked into the repo'
Plan: 'pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in'
pyproject.toml [dev] does not list pip-tools
Makefile has GIT_CLIFF_VERSION pinned and install instructions; no equivalent for pip-tools
docs/releasing.md Pinning section AC requires 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' as a literal substring — but the tool's install method is unspecified
findings.finding: The plan designates `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (from pip-tools) as the tool to generate `.github/release-requirements.txt`, but provides no install path for `pip-tools` itself — neither in `pyproject.toml [dev]` nor as a pinned standalone tool (unlike `git-cliff`, which has an explicit `pipx install git-cliff==<version>` instruction in `docs/releasing.md` and a version pin in the Makefile). The maintainer has no documented, reproducible way to regenerate the lock file.
findings.location: Scope section / .github/release-requirements.in + docs/releasing.md Pinning section AC
scenarios: A maintainer needs to regenerate the lock (e.g. after bumping build/Hatchling/Twine) but has no documented install path for pip-tools, leading to ad-hoc version selection and reproducibility drift.
CI cannot regenerate the lock (pip-tools not installed), so the lock-regeneration step in docs is only locally executable by whoever has pip-tools installed.
findings.suggested_fix: Following the git-cliff pattern: add `PIP_TOOLS_VERSION := <exact>` to the Makefile, add a `Makefile` target or note in `docs/releasing.md` like `pipx install pip-tools==$(PIP_TOOLS_VERSION)`, and check the installed version before running `pip-compile` (as the `changelog` target does for git-cliff).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the venv prescription in the What section with the AC and Scope: either add an explicit AC criterion verifying that release.yml creates a fresh venv before `pip install --require-hashes`, or revise the What section to drop the venv requirement and document why the guard script alone suffices without a venv.
evidence: What section: 'build in a fresh venv containing only the hash-locked packages, then run a small guard script (scripts/check_build_env_locked.py)'
AC bullet 4: 'pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt re-hashes every artifact against the lock and fails the build if any package was replaced' — no venv mentioned
Scope section: lists 'scripts/check_build_env_locked.py (new)' but does not list venv creation as a release.yml step deliverable
ABSENCE: no AC criterion requires or verifies that a venv is created before the hash-locked install
findings.finding: The What section prescribes building inside 'a fresh venv containing only the hash-locked packages' to eliminate ambient-package risk under --no-isolation, but this venv creation step is absent from the Scope (not listed as a workflow step deliverable) and absent from the AC (AC4 only specifies 'pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt' with no venv). An implementer reading the AC could install into the runner's system environment and rely solely on the guard script, which is a weaker guarantee than the venv the What section prescribes — sending the implementer in two directions on the core build-isolation mechanism.
findings.location: What section ('build in a fresh venv') vs AC bullet 4 (pip install, no venv) vs Scope (scripts/check_build_env_locked.py listed, venv not listed)
scenarios: Implementer installs hash-locked packages into the system Python env on ubuntu-latest, which already contains pip/setuptools/wheel/packaging; the guard script's allowlist handles those but other ambient packages (e.g. a pre-installed cryptography version from the runner image) may silently satisfy --no-isolation's import-time resolution without being in the lock.
Reviewer approving the AC-passing implementation may not notice the missing venv, leaving the ambient-package hole the What section explicitly aimed to close.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 4 (after the pip install sentence): 'The install runs inside a freshly created venv (`python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate`) so no runner-image ambient packages are resolvable; the guard script's allowlist is a secondary defence, not the primary isolation boundary.' Also add 'venv creation step before hash-locked install' to the Scope's release.yml bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State an idempotency strategy for the actionlint Makefile prerequisite (e.g., check if the binary already exists at the expected path and its SHA-256 matches before downloading; only download if absent or mismatched), and specify how `make lint` handles a network failure during the download (fail with a clear infrastructure-error message distinct from a lint error).
evidence: Plan: 'actionlint is a Go binary — install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite (dogfooding the same digest-verify discipline this story establishes for mcp-publisher)'
gerrit-verify.yaml line 302: '- name: lint (ruff check + format --check, gating)\n  run: make lint' — a plain run step with no preceding actionlint download step
Makefile has no network-fetching targets; every tool is installed via pip or is a pre-installed system binary
The mcp-publisher download (the dogfooded pattern) runs only at release time, not at every pre-merge lint — so frequency and reliability requirements differ materially
findings.finding: The plan installs actionlint via 'a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite' — meaning `make lint` triggers a runtime network download during the Gerrit Verified gate. The current Makefile has no network-fetching prerequisites; this introduces a novel infrastructure-failure mode (transient GitHub release endpoint unavailability breaks `make lint`) into the most critical pre-merge gate. The plan states no idempotency strategy (e.g., skip download if binary already present and verified) or failure handling for this step.
findings.location: Scope section — Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] bullet; What section — 'actionlint is a Go binary — install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite'
scenarios: GitHub releases endpoint is transiently unavailable: every contributor's Gerrit change gets Verified -1, blocking all merges until the infrastructure recovers

actionlint binary is already downloaded and verified from a previous run on the same runner: the Makefile prereq re-downloads and re-verifies it unnecessarily on every lint invocation (unless idempotency is built in)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Makefile prerequisite design: check for `$(ACTIONLINT_BIN)` existence + digest before fetching; emit a clear `::error::actionlint binary download failed — infrastructure issue, recheck` message on failure (parallel to the mcp-publisher and pip-audit retry/error patterns already in release.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml). Document the install path so that CI environments can pre-warm the binary (e.g., a one-time cache step).

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fixture test case to the `check_build_env_locked.py` unit test that supplies a freeze file where an installed package has a version differing from the lock (e.g., lock: `foo==1.0`, freeze: `foo==1.1`) and asserts the script exits non-zero.
evidence: AC states the script 'fails on any installed package absent from the lock or version-mismatched'
Test description says only: 'a unit test feeds the script a lock+freeze fixture pair to confirm it passes on a clean env and fails on an extra package'
ABSENCE: no fixture or assertion described for the version-mismatch branch (e.g., lock says foo==1.0, freeze shows foo==1.1 — should fail, but no test covers this)
findings.finding: The unit test described for `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` covers only the 'extra package' failure case but omits the 'version-mismatched package' failure path, even though the script's contract (and AC) explicitly requires failure on both.
findings.location: AC item 5 (check_build_env_locked.py) and Scope section: 'tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py'
scenarios: A compromised package replaces itself at the correct version name but a different build — the version-mismatch branch is the primary defense against version-drift attacks, yet it goes untested
A transient resolver bug installs a mismatched version; the guard passes silently because only the 'extra package' path was validated by tests
findings.suggested_fix: In the unit test, add a third fixture scenario: lock contains `foo==1.0 \\ --hash=sha256:abc`, freeze contains `foo==1.1`; assert `check_build_env_locked.py` returns a non-zero exit code with a message identifying `foo` as version-mismatched. This exercises the `version != expected` branch independently from the `name not in lock` branch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether `go install github.com/rhysd/actionlint/cmd/actionlint@v<exact-version>` (pinned via go.sum) or a pip-installable actionlint wrapper satisfies the same supply-chain guarantee with less Makefile complexity, and document the chosen approach's caching/idempotency behaviour in the plan.
evidence: Plan: 'actionlint is a Go binary — install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite (dogfooding the same digest-verify discipline this story establishes for mcp-publisher)'
No consideration of established installation paths: actionlint-action (rhysd/actionlint-action), brew, go install github.com/rhysd/actionlint/cmd/actionlint@v<exact>, or pip wrapper packages
The plan does not specify caching, install prefix, or how CI avoids re-downloading on every make lint invocation
findings.finding: The plan mandates `actionlint` installation via a 'pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite' — a novel per-repo binary-bootstrapping mechanism. Established prior art (the official `actionlint-action`, `brew install actionlint`, `go install`, the rhysd/actionlint docker image, or a pip-installable wrapper) is not considered. The Makefile download approach introduces unanswered questions: caching behaviour (re-downloads on every `make lint`?), PATH visibility in CI vs. local, and whether the download itself must be bootstrapped from a trusted source — exactly the supply-chain risk this story aims to eliminate.
findings.location: Scope section: 'actionlint is a Go binary — install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite'
scenarios: Every developer `make lint` invocation triggers a network download, slowing local development and creating a hard dependency on GitHub release availability
The Makefile download logic becomes its own pinned-binary bootstrapping problem (curl | sha256sum pattern) with no caching, requiring the same scrutiny as the mcp-publisher pattern it mimics
findings.suggested_fix: If the Makefile download approach is retained, specify: (a) install to a `.tools/` or `$(GOPATH)/bin` directory, (b) a cache key / idempotency guard (`test -f .tools/actionlint && .tools/actionlint --version | grep <pinned> || download`), and (c) add the download URL + SHA-256 to the same `release-requirements.txt` or a parallel `tool-requirements.txt` so it is reviewed in one place.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the OIDC job-level isolation rationale (why step-ordering is insufficient, why structural job split is required) in a persistent project doc — e.g., an ADR under `docs/adr/`, a `SECURITY.md` note, or a dedicated subsection in `docs/releasing.md` beyond 'Pinning' — so the constraint survives the plan.
evidence: 'Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'
'Security contract — structural job split (not step ordering). This follows GitHub's own Security hardening for GitHub Actions guidance'
No ADR or CLAUDE.md update is listed in Scope or ACs
findings.finding: The OIDC job-split architecture decision — that `id-token: write` is a job-level permission reachable from the first step, requiring structural job isolation rather than step ordering — is a novel, non-obvious security architecture choice documented only in the plan narrative. Post-merge the plan is not a persistent artifact; `docs/releasing.md` is explicitly scoped to 'Pinning' only, and no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is specified, leaving no durable home for this rationale.
findings.location: Scope / `docs/releasing.md` section ownership; 'Security contract — structural job split' subsection
scenarios: A future maintainer sees two jobs where one would do and merges them back into one, re-introducing the OIDC co-residency vulnerability, because the architectural rationale is not in any project doc.
A code reviewer unfamiliar with GitHub's job-level OIDC scoping approves the consolidation without understanding the security implication.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Release security architecture' (or 'OIDC isolation') subsection to `docs/releasing.md` (or a `docs/adr/NNN-oidc-job-split.md`) explaining: (1) GitHub Actions OIDC token is job-scoped and available from step 1, (2) step ordering within one job cannot prevent a compromised download from minting a token, (3) therefore `mcp_verify` must have no `id-token: write` and `mcp_registry` must `needs: mcp_verify` and consume only the verified artifact. Update the Scope to list this section as a story deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' section or a Makefile comment) that `zizmor` must be kept at the same version in both `pyproject.toml [dev]` and `.github/release-requirements.txt`, and add a `make lint` or CI check that asserts the two pins match (e.g., extract both versions and `diff` them, or use a single source of truth like a variable in `release-requirements.in` that `pip-compile` propagates).
evidence: 'zizmor is pip-installable — pin it in pyproject.toml [dev] (and hash-pin it in the release lock for the release-lint usage)'
No sync mechanism or update-both instruction is mentioned in Scope, ACs, or docs/releasing.md spec
findings.finding: `zizmor` is pinned in two places — `pyproject.toml [dev]` (for `make lint` in the dev environment) and `.github/release-requirements.txt` (for the release-lint usage) — but the plan specifies no mechanism or documented process for keeping these two pins in sync. A developer updating one pin but not the other could silently diverge the linting behavior between dev and release contexts.
findings.location: Scope: 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]' and '.github/release-requirements.txt' bullets; AC line 3
scenarios: A maintainer runs `pip-compile` to update build tools, bumping `zizmor` in the release lock, but forgets to update `pyproject.toml [dev]`. CI uses the dev version; the release pipeline uses a different (possibly more permissive) version.
The reverse: `pyproject.toml [dev]` is updated via a dependency PR but the release lock is not regenerated, so `make lint` in dev passes but the release-lint step still uses an old version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --connect-timeout 30` (or equivalent) to both the `curl` invocation for the MCP publisher archive and the `actionlint` Makefile download, and document that a non-zero exit after retries is a hard failure requiring re-trigger.
evidence: Plan explicitly specifies the three-step curl invocation: '1. `curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`' with no `--retry` or `--connect-timeout` flag
Plan specifies actionlint 'install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite' — no retry mentioned
Both are blocking steps in a release pipeline where a human re-trigger is the only recovery
ABSENCE: no `--retry N --retry-delay M --connect-timeout T` flags or equivalent retry wrapper specified anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `curl` download of the MCP publisher archive is specified as a single invocation (`curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`) with no retry, backoff, or timeout flag. Transient network failures (CDN blip, GitHub releases throttle) during a production release run would fail the entire release workflow with no recovery path short of a full manual re-trigger. The same applies to the `actionlint` binary download in the Makefile prerequisite, which is also described as a pinned-version GitHub release download with no retry.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute'; Scope section — Makefile prerequisite for actionlint; AC line: 'curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz'
scenarios: GitHub CDN returns a transient 503 during a time-sensitive release; the sha256sum step never runs; the release fails and must be re-triggered manually
GitHub rate-limits the unauthenticated actionlint download during a CI surge; `make lint` fails before any code is checked, blocking the Gerrit Verified gate
findings.suggested_fix: Change step 1 to: `curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --connect-timeout 30 "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`. Apply the same flags to the actionlint Makefile download. Document in `docs/releasing.md` that persistent curl failure (after retries) requires a manual re-trigger and checking GitHub's status page.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` tests a rebar-specific invariant (e.g. the hash constant value in `release.yml` matches the actual pinned archive) rather than just verifying that `sha256sum` rejects a corrupted file — if it is only the latter, fold the check into `test_release_workflow_pins.py` as a static assertion that the hash field is a non-empty 64-hex-char string, or drop it entirely.
evidence: Plan: 'writes a test copy of the pinned archive, corrupts one byte, runs the mcp_verify sha256sum -c logic, and asserts a non-zero exit'
ABSENCE: no existing test in tests/unit/ tests sha256sum platform behavior (search returned zero hits for test_mcp_publisher)

The plan already mandates test_release_workflow_pins.py to assert the sha256sum STEP IS WIRED into release.yml (AC 6) — the correctness of the wiring is already gated; testing sha256sum itself is redundant relative to the risk
findings.finding: `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` proposes writing a test copy of a pinned archive, corrupting one byte, and asserting `sha256sum -c` exits nonzero. This tests a well-known, stable platform utility (`sha256sum`) rather than any project-specific logic — the real supply-chain risk is an incorrect hash VALUE in the workflow, not `sha256sum` malfunctioning. No equivalent 'test a platform tool works' test exists elsewhere in the codebase (the sha256sum usages in external-integration.yml and reconcile-bridge.yml have no companion tests). This is a low-YAGNI test that adds a subprocess-spawning integration concern to the unit suite without verifying any rebar-specific invariant.
findings.location: Scope section — `tests/unit/test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` (new); AC bullet 6
scenarios: The test passes vacuously because sha256sum always works, giving false assurance that the pinned hash value in the workflow is correct
The test introduces a subprocess invocation of sha256sum into the unit suite, adding a platform-tool dependency to tests/unit/ not present in any other unit test

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing a bespoke Makefile download mechanism for `actionlint`, confirm whether `actionlint-py` (the PyPI wrapper) is acceptable; if so, add it to `pyproject.toml [dev]` alongside `zizmor` to keep a single tool-install pattern, and remove the custom Makefile prerequisite.
evidence: Plan: 'actionlint is a Go binary — install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite (dogfooding the same digest-verify discipline)'
pyproject.toml [dev] already declares: ruff==0.15.19, pytest-xdist==3.8.0, pytest-split==0.11.0 — pinned exact versions via pyproject.toml, not bespoke download scripts
Plan installs zizmor via 'pyproject.toml [dev]' (pip-installable) but proposes a separate mechanism for actionlint despite both being linters
Makefile currently has NO download-and-verify mechanism for any tool (git-cliff uses pipx, documented separately); adding one is a new pattern with no Rule-of-Three support in this repo
findings.finding: The plan installs `actionlint` (a Go binary) via a custom 'pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite', yet `actionlint` is available on PyPI as the `actionlint-py` wrapper package — the same integration path the plan already uses for `zizmor`. Introducing a bespoke download-and-verify mechanism in the Makefile when a pip-installable wrapper exists diverges from the established `pyproject.toml [dev]` pattern used for every other dev tool in the repo (ruff, mypy, pytest, zizmor itself), creating a second tool-install pattern where one already covers the need.
findings.location: Scope section — Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] bullet; AC bullet 3 ('actionlint installed via a pinned-version release download with SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite')
scenarios: CI environments that install via `pip install -e '.[dev]'` get zizmor automatically but not actionlint, requiring additional Makefile prerequisite invocation — increasing contributor friction and CI setup complexity
The Makefile download-and-verify mechanism is a new code surface to maintain (version pin, SHA-256, platform detection) vs a one-line pyproject.toml entry
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"actionlint-py>=<version>"` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` (exact-pinned, same pattern as ruff/pytest-xdist), then invoke `actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml` in the `lint` Makefile target — no custom download logic needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify zizmor's `excessive-permissions` check catches `id-token: write` declared at the **job** level in a multi-job workflow; if it does not, add a `test_release_workflow_pins.py` assertion (check (e)) that explicitly fails if `mcp_verify` gains `id-token: write` — which the plan already describes but should be confirmed as the fallback rather than assumed to be redundant.
evidence: Plan: 'zizmor enforces the generic checks: full-SHA pinning of every uses:, persist-credentials: false, and over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC)'
The mcp_verify/mcp_registry job split is the primary architectural control; zizmor is asserted as the enforcement backstop for re-introduction of id-token in mcp_verify
zizmor's excessive-permissions check behavior on job-level vs workflow-level permissions in a multi-job workflow is not documented in the plan
findings.finding: The plan asserts `zizmor` flags 'over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC)' on `release.yml`, but whether zizmor's `excessive-permissions` check specifically targets `id-token: write` at the job level (vs. workflow level) is unverified. The plan's mcp_verify/mcp_registry split is the primary security control, but it relies on zizmor catching any future re-introduction of `id-token: write` in mcp_verify. If zizmor does not fire on job-level OIDC permissions in this configuration, the structural guarantee could erode silently.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 6: 'zizmor enforces ... over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC)'
scenarios: A future change adds id-token: write to mcp_verify (e.g., during a merge conflict resolution); zizmor does not flag it; the OIDC isolation guarantee is silently broken
findings.suggested_fix: The plan's test_release_workflow_pins.py check (e) already asserts the permission structure, so confirming it is the definitive enforcement path (not merely belt-and-suspenders to zizmor) would resolve this finding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add checks (a) and (b) to the test list in the Scope (e.g. '(a) covered by zizmor — not in this test; (b) covered by zizmor — not in this test') or renumber the checks starting at (a) so there is no unexplained gap in the labeling.
evidence: Scope: 'parsing release.yml + docs/releasing.md: (c) the build invocation includes --no-isolation; (d) the docs/releasing.md...; (e) the job-permission split...; (f) the release-evidence wiring...; (g) a chmod +x mcp-publisher step...; (h) the ambient-package guard step'
ABSENCE: no (a) or (b) check is defined anywhere in the plan for this test file
findings.finding: The Scope section's list of test_release_workflow_pins.py checks is labeled (c)-(h), with no (a) or (b) defined anywhere in the plan. The AC section references these same labels. The gap leaves an implementer unsure whether checks (a) and (b) were intentionally omitted (e.g. because zizmor covers them) or accidentally dropped during editing.
findings.location: Scope section: 'tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py (new)' bullet — check labels (c)-(h); AC bullet 7 similarly references check (f)
scenarios: Implementer searches for checks (a) and (b) to understand the full test scope and finds nothing — may add spurious checks or ask for clarification unnecessarily.
During review, a reviewer notices the gap and raises a question, slowing the change.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope's test bullet, prepend: '(a) and (b) are enforced by zizmor (SHA-pins and persist-credentials) and are therefore excluded from this unit test; rebar-specific checks start at (c):' — then keep the existing (c)-(h) list unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope's `docs/releasing.md` deliverable an explicit instruction to audit and update any existing content that references the patterns being replaced (`--upgrade build/twine`, mutable action tags, `releases/latest` MCP URL) so no stale guidance survives the change.
evidence: 'docs/releasing.md — document the pinning policy and the pin-update process. Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'

The 'Why' section explicitly names the replaced patterns: '--upgrade build :37', '--upgrade twine :41', 'pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1 (:65)' — no corresponding audit of releasing.md for references to these
findings.finding: The plan scopes `docs/releasing.md` changes exclusively to adding a new 'Pinning' section, but does not specify auditing the rest of `docs/releasing.md` for stale references to the patterns being replaced (e.g., `pip install --upgrade build`, `--upgrade twine`, `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1`). If existing doc content references those old patterns, it will be stranded by this change.
findings.location: Scope: `docs/releasing.md` bullet; AC 'Pinning section' item
scenarios: A releaser follows `docs/releasing.md` and sees a step saying `pip install --upgrade twine` (old content), contradicting the new hash-locked install. They follow the old doc and bypass the lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend `test_release_workflow_pins.py`) to assert that the `Makefile` lint target invokes `zizmor` with the explicit path argument `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (not a glob or directory), so a future accidental broadening of scope is caught before it breaks the Verified gate.
evidence: .pre-commit-config.yaml:30: `entry: make lint` with `pass_filenames: false` and `types_or: [python, pyi]`
gerrit-verify.yaml:311: `pre-commit run --all-files --show-diff-on-failure`
Plan: 'invoked with `release.yml` as an explicit path argument (not repo-wide)'
Plan: 'so they run under the Gerrit `Verified` gate'

The pre-commit hook triggers on `types_or: [python, pyi]`, so `make lint` with zizmor scoped to release.yml will NOT trigger on YAML-only changes — confirmed. The --all-files CI run is fine because explicit path ensures only release.yml is checked.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s header claim of comprehensive SHA-pinning is contradicted only by line 75 (`require-ticket` job's `setup-python@v6`), but the file's `require-ticket` job at line 75 is a first-party actions/ step, while other jobs in gerrit-verify.yaml use SHA-pins for the same action at lines 141 and 410. The plan correctly notes line 75 as out-of-scope. However, the plan's scoping justification rests on the premise that running `zizmor`/`actionlint` scoped to `release.yml` (not `gerrit-verify.yaml`) avoids breaking the Verified gate. This is sound IF the pre-commit hook (`.pre-commit-config.yaml:30: entry: make lint`) does not also pick up the changed `make lint` command and invoke `zizmor gerrit-verify.yaml` during commits touching that file. The `.pre-commit-config.yaml` hook entry is `make lint` with `pass_filenames: false` and `types_or: [python, pyi]` — so it triggers on Python file changes only, not on `.yaml` changes. However, CI runs `pre-commit run --all-files` which scans ALL files including YAML. If `make lint` calls `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (explicit path), only that file is checked even in `--all-files` mode. The plan's scoping is therefore sound, but this dependency on the explicit-path invocation being the ONLY usage is an unverified premise given the pre-commit `--all-files` CI step.
findings.location: Scope section / Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] bullet; pre-commit CI at gerrit-verify.yaml:311
scenarios: A contributor modifies gerrit-verify.yaml only — pre-commit hook does NOT fire (types_or filters to Python). CI `--all-files` fires `make lint` which calls `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` only. So gerrit-verify.yaml's pre-existing findings do NOT surface. Scoping is robust.

However: if an implementer accidentally drops the explicit path arg and uses `zizmor .github/workflows/` or `zizmor .`, the Verified gate immediately breaks on the pre-existing gerrit-verify.yaml:75 finding — the plan's scoping rationale hinges entirely on the explicit-path invocation being preserved exactly as specified.
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, add a check that reads `Makefile` and asserts the string `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (not `zizmor .github/` or `zizmor`) appears in the lint target body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` doc-AC check to assert that all three required substrings appear within a contiguous 'Pinning' section (e.g., by checking the section header `## Pinning` exists AND the substrings follow it), rather than grepping the whole file — so pre-existing text in other sections cannot satisfy the check.
evidence: docs/releasing.md line 14: 'published with the `mcp-publisher` CLI' — 'mcp-publisher' appears in existing text
docs/releasing.md section 4 heading: 'Update the MCP Registry — automated (OIDC in CI)' uses mcp-publisher throughout
Plan AC: 'verified by `test_release_workflow_pins.py` asserting all three exact substrings are present in `docs/releasing.md`: `pip-compile --generate-hashes`, `MCP publisher`, and `full-SHA`'

Search for 'MCP publisher' (with capital P and space) in docs/releasing.md: the existing text uses lowercase 'mcp-publisher' (hyphenated) rather than 'MCP publisher' (space-separated with caps) — ambiguous match depending on exact casing
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `docs/releasing.md`'s 'Pinning' section will be verified by `test_release_workflow_pins.py` asserting three exact substrings: `pip-compile --generate-hashes`, `MCP publisher`, and `full-SHA`. However, the current `docs/releasing.md` already contains a reference to 'MCP publisher' in its existing section 4 ('Update the MCP Registry') — specifically in prose about `mcp-publisher login github-oidc`. A search confirms this. The AC's `grep -q 'MCP publisher'` check will therefore PASS even if the dedicated 'Pinning' section is never actually written, because the existing section 4 text already satisfies that single substring.
findings.location: AC: `docs/releasing.md` contains a 'Pinning' section / `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (d)
scenarios: If 'MCP publisher' (space, capitalized) is used as the grep target and the existing text only has 'mcp-publisher' (hyphen, lowercase), the test correctly fails until the Pinning section is added. But if the implementer uses a case-insensitive grep or the existing text happens to match, the test may pass without the Pinning section existing.

The substring 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' and 'full-SHA' are genuinely absent from the current docs/releasing.md, so those two checks do anchor the Pinning section requirement. Only 'MCP publisher' is ambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the 'Pinning' section's text between its `## Pinning` header and the next `##` header, then assert all three substrings appear within that extracted block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` as an impacted consumer: if `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` is ever referenced in `test.yml`, the parity test's `_INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES` set or `gerrit-verify.yaml` must be updated accordingly; add a note to the scope or AC.
evidence: test_ci_workflow_parity.py:79: scripts_in_test = set(re.findall(r"scripts/[A-Za-z0-9_]+\.py", test_yml))
test_ci_workflow_parity.py:81: missing = scripts_in_test - scripts_in_gerrit - _INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES
Plan scope: 'scripts/check_build_env_locked.py (new)' — wired into release.yml; but scope does not enumerate test_ci_workflow_parity.py as an impacted file
findings.finding: The plan adds `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` to the scope but does not enumerate `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py::test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci` as a consumer. That test uses a regex `scripts/[A-Za-z0-9_]+\.py` to grep `test.yml` and demands every matched script also appear in `gerrit-verify.yaml`. If an implementer wires `check_build_env_locked.py` into `test.yml` (e.g. as a standalone gate), the parity test will fail unless it also appears in `gerrit-verify.yaml` or is added to `_INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES`.
findings.location: Scope section / scripts/check_build_env_locked.py + tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py
scenarios: Implementer also adds check_build_env_locked.py invocation to test.yml as a local sanity gate → parity test fires → make test breaks → Gerrit Verified -1 until gerrit-verify.yaml or _INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES is updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'If `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` is referenced from `test.yml`, add it to `gerrit-verify.yaml` or to `_INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES` in `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` with a rationale (release-only script, not a pre-merge drift gate).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases` publishes a `checksums.txt` (or equivalent) alongside the `mcp-publisher` binaries before drafting the implementation; if none exists, simplify the plan to 'hard-code the SHA-256 at pin time' and remove the conditional 'prefer upstream' branch.
evidence: Plan: 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time with a rationale comment.'
Existing release.yml:129 downloads from releases/latest with no checksums reference — no upstream checksums file is currently in use.
search_files for 'checksums.txt' returns no matches anywhere in the repo.
Evidence class: Unverified — upstream release asset inventory not confirmable from this environment.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Prefer an upstream `checksums.txt` if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one' for the MCP publisher archive digest. There is no evidence in the repo or plan that such a file exists on the `modelcontextprotocol/registry` releases page. The current `release.yml:129` downloads from `releases/latest` with no checksums reference. If the implementer checks for a checksums file that does not exist, they may spend time on a dead branch, or (worse) defer pin-time SHA computation assuming upstream will provide it.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one'
scenarios: Implementer spends time checking for an upstream checksums.txt that does not exist, then falls back to hard-coding — time wasted but no functional gap.
Implementer incorrectly assumes upstream ships checksums and does not hard-code the SHA-256, leaving the verify step empty or conditional.
findings.suggested_fix: Check the modelcontextprotocol/registry releases page for a checksums file. If absent (likely), replace 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one; otherwise hard-code' with the unconditional 'Hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time with a rationale comment and a note to re-verify on each publisher version bump.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit minimal `permissions:` block (e.g. `permissions: {}` or `permissions: {actions: read}`) for the evidence-consolidation job in the plan, so the job's access posture is intentional and `zizmor`-verifiable rather than inherited from workflow-level grants.
evidence: Plan introduces a 'final aggregation step downloads all fragments and concatenates them into one release-evidence.txt' but specifies no `permissions:` for that job

Plan says zizmor checks 'over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC)' — but zizmor flags over-broad grants it sees; it cannot require a permissions block be added to a job the author hasn't written yet
ABSENCE: no explicit `permissions: {}` or `permissions: {contents: read}` declared for the consolidation job anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The consolidation job (which downloads all evidence fragments and concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt`) is introduced by the plan but its job-level permissions are never declared. If the workflow's top-level `permissions` block grants `id-token: write` (or other elevated grants) and no per-job `permissions:` block overrides it, the consolidation job inherits OIDC token access it does not need. `zizmor` detects violations of declared permissions but cannot enforce a declaration that is absent from a newly introduced job.
findings.location: Scope section / AC item for evidence pipeline; 'Print and retain release evidence' section
scenarios: Workflow-level `permissions: id-token: write` (needed by `mcp_registry`) propagates to the consolidation job, giving it an unnecessary OIDC token; a compromised aggregation step could mint a registry token
A future author adds `contents: write` at workflow level for artifact upload and the consolidation job silently acquires write access to the repo
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `permissions: {}` stanza (or the minimal set actually required, e.g. `actions: read` for `download-artifact`) to the consolidation job definition in `release.yml`, and add a `test_release_workflow_pins.py` assertion that the consolidation job carries no `id-token: write` permission (mirroring the existing assertion for `mcp_verify`).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
